sites.one_line: Maranguka is an Aboriginal-led, place-based justice reinvestment initiative in Bourke, NSW, operating Australia's first major justice reinvestment site through the Bourke Tribal Council's "Growing Our Kids Up Safe, Smart and Strong" strategy.

history.event: Just Reinvest NSW began as a campaign to bring justice reinvestment to NSW.
Partnership between Just Reinvest NSW and the Bourke community / Maranguka commenced.
Bourke became the first major pilot site in Australia to adapt and implement an Aboriginal-led place-based model of justice reinvestment, the Maranguka Justice Reinvestment Project.
The 'Growing Our Kids Up Safe, Smart and Strong' strategy was developed through community conversations using data on Aboriginal young people's lives in Bourke.
KPMG published the Maranguka Justice Reinvestment Project Impact Assessment (November 2018), reporting 2017 outcomes.
Just Reinvest NSW and Maranguka transitioned from being initiatives of the Aboriginal Legal Service to operating as independent not-for-profit organisations (media release 5 April 2022).

Maranguka made submissions to the NSW Parliament Inquiry into Community Safety in Regional and Rural Communities (31 May 2024) and the Senate Inquiry into Australia's youth justice and incarceration system (10 October 2024); published its 2024 Annual Report.

programs: Growing Our Kids Up Safe, Smart and Strong strategy
Maranguka Community Hub
Bourke Tribal Council governance model
Justice 'circuit breakers' (bail breach reduction, outstanding warrants, learner driver / driver licensing program)
Strategic Working Groups (Early Childhood and Parenting, 8-18 year olds, Role of Women, Role of Men)
Palimaa interactive data platform
Life Stage Dashboard

sites.notes: VERIFIED figures: all six KPMG percentage/dollar metrics (23% DV, 31% Yr12, 38% juvenile charges, 14% bail breaches, 42% days in custody, $3.1m gross 2017, ~5x return, $7m/5yr projection) were extracted verbatim from the primary-source KPMG PDF (Nov 2018) on the Indigenous Justice Clearinghouse, not just secondary commentary. IMPORTANT CAVEAT carried in the report itself: 'The impact assessment is not an outcomes evaluation and is based on a single year of change' (2017 vs 2016) — these figures should not be presented as a sustained/causal trend. Logo URL is a real on-site asset (Maranguka-Logo.svg) referenced in the homepage; not independently rendered-checked but path is consistent with the site's WP media library. confirmed_website maranguka.org.au is the live, project-specific homepage. COULD NOT confirm: current (2026) CEO/leadership beyond Alister Ferguson OAM as founder/ED — Matt Davidson and Geoffrey Winters are from the 2022 ALS media release and may be outdated; the 2024 Annual Report PDF was located but not fully parsed for current board/staff or newer metrics, so leadership names carry a recency risk. CULTURAL SENSITIVITY: Maranguka is Ngemba/Wangkumara word for 'caring for others'; this is an Aboriginal-led, community-owned initiative governed by the Bourke Tribal Council — attribution of outcomes should foreground community leadership, not external partners (KPMG/Just Reinvest were evaluators/conveners). No deceased persons were named. 'Alister' spelling per maranguka.org.au; the ALS release spells it 'Alistair' — site spelling preferred. Note name variance 'Alister' vs 'Alistair' across sources.

sites.one_line: Moree Justice Reinvestment is a community-led, data-informed initiative supported by Just Reinvest NSW that works with the Aboriginal community in Moree, NSW, to develop local solutions reducing young people's contact with the criminal justice system.

history.event: Just Reinvest NSW and Maranguka transitioned from operating under the Aboriginal Legal Service (NSW/ACT) to become independent not-for-profit organisations; media release notes Just Reinvest NSW had partnered with communities in Bourke, Moree and Mount Druitt on local justice reinvestment projects (5 April 2022).
Moree Youth Forum and Block Party held in April 2022, with nearly 800 young people attending; Moree Youth Forum Report published March 2022.
'Redefining Reinvestment' report published (the Reinvestment Forum report), based on research with Aboriginal communities in Bourke, Moree and Mt Druitt.

NSW Government (Premier Chris Minns and Minister for Youth Justice Jihad Dib) announced more than $2 million in additional youth-justice funding for Moree, building on an initial $13.4 million targeted response; Safe Aboriginal Youth (SAY) and Casework Support contracts commenced April 2025.

programs: Youth Bail Project (bail-condition review with the Aboriginal Legal Service)
Moree Youth Forum and Block Party (April 2022)

sites.notes:
Moree Justice Reinvestment is not a separately incorporated organisation; it is a community-led initiative supported by Just Reinvest NSW (the parent org, independent since 5 April 2022), with a local office at 4/96 Balo St, Moree NSW 2400 (moreeinfo@justreinvest.org.au). The confirmed_website is the Moree community page on the Just Reinvest NSW site; the logo is Just Reinvest NSW's (a white horizontal SVG, verified to return HTTP 200) — there is no Moree-specific logo.

IMPORTANT ATTRIBUTION CAVEAT: The largest dollar figures ($13.4M targeted response, $2M+ additional, $1M after-hours, SAY program, Casework Support, Operation Mongoose's 255 offenders/75 ringleaders, 30+ police surge operations) come from NSW Government / Youth Justice NSW media releases describing a whole-of-government 'targeted response' to youth crime in Moree. These are RELATED to but NOT the same as Just Reinvest NSW's community-led justice reinvestment initiative — some (notably the heavy-policing 'surge operations' and Operation Mongoose) reflect an enforcement approach distinct from, and arguably in tension with, the JR philosophy. They are included with exact quotes and government source URLs but should be presented as government-program context, not as JR's own measured outcomes. The $175k/18 community grants and $1M after-hours activity (delivered by Aboriginal orgs SHAE Academy, Miyay Birray, PCYC) are closer to the community-led model.

COULD NOT FIND: a Moree-specific independent evaluation comparable to the 2018 KPMG Maranguka (Bourke) impact assessment; no published before/after crime-reduction percentages, bail-compliance rates, or dollars-returned figures attributable specifically to the Moree JR initiative. No publicly named Moree local coordinator, chairperson, or Aboriginal community-leadership-group members were found on the org's own pages (only the parent-org CEO Geoff Scott and Communications Manager James Nichols are publicly named) — local Aboriginal leaders deliberately not named here, both because none were sourced and out of cultural-safety caution. Exact founding/launch year of the Moree initiative is not stated on the site; partnership with Moree is documented from at least the November 2022 'Redefining Reinvestment' research and the April 2022 Youth Forum. CULTURAL FLAG: this is an Aboriginal-community-led initiative; any public-facing use should foreground community voice and avoid framing the policing/enforcement figures as 'justice reinvestment success'.

sites.one_line: Mount Druitt Justice Reinvestment is a community-led Just Reinvest NSW site on Darug Country in Western Sydney that runs youth-led programs (including Mounty Yarns, OzTag and a Learner Driver Mentors Program) to reduce contact between Aboriginal young people and the criminal justice system.

history.event: Just Reinvest NSW began as a justice reinvestment initiative, auspiced by Aboriginal Legal Service (NSW/ACT) Ltd, with a coalition of more than 20 organisations.

The NSW Government expanded its Justice Reinvestment strategy to Mt Druitt in early 2024, supporting Just Reinvest NSW's collaboration with the Aboriginal community to set up a new justice reinvestment site; four communities received funding over three years from June 2024 to June 2027 under the Justice Reinvestment Grants Program.

Mounty Aboriginal Youth & Community Services (MAYCS) transitioned out of JR NSW Mt Druitt and registered as an independent, community-controlled entity with an independent board; the team grew from five to 12 full-time local Aboriginal staff.

sites.notes:
Mount Druitt is one of several Just Reinvest NSW community sites (on Darug Country, Western Sydney). The confirmed_website is the program's dedicated page; the org homepage is https://www.justreinvest.org.au/. Logo (HTTP 200, image/svg+xml) and hero image (https://www.justreinvest.org.au/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/mount-druitt_new.jpg, HTTP 200) both verified to resolve.

Impact metrics are MODERATE: the richest figures are funding/staffing/participation from the Dusseldorp Forum article (April 30, 2024), all quoted verbatim with source. I did NOT find a Maranguka-style independent evaluation with crime-reduction percentages specific to Mt Druitt (the canonical 2018 KPMG figures belong to Bourke/Maranguka, not Mt Druitt) — this site is newer (established early 2024) so outcome evaluations likely do not yet exist publicly. Could not find an exact dollar figure for the per-community share of the $7.5m grants program, only the MAYCS-secured $3.3m total.

Cultural-sensitivity flags: (1) People listed are publicly named in org/funder materials as staff, coordinators, or youth ambassadors who chose public advocacy roles; no deceased persons named. (2) Several leaders are young people with lived experience of the justice system who self-identify publicly via Mounty Yarns — they are named because they are public advocates, but downstream display should preserve that framing of self-determination rather than recast them as case studies.

A note for internal JusticeHub use only (not from a public source): project memory previously flagged "Mounty Yarns REMOVED" as a JusticeHub anchor-community decision — that is an internal map-curation note and does NOT indicate the program ceased. The public record shows Mounty Yarns as an active, named program/film/report; reported as such.

Confirmed website is the Cowra Information and Neighbourhood Centre (CINC) homepage (cinc.org.au); the provided source_url was a generic AG.gov index page. There is NO dedicated 'Cowra Justice Reinvestment' website or program page; the CINC homepage itself does not mention justice reinvestment at all (it lists Youth, Aged, Family, Community Builders and Disability services). Logo URL verified live (HTTP 200, image/jpeg).

COULD NOT FIND: a site-specific funding figure for Cowra (only the program-wide $79M/$109M is public); any outcome/impact metrics for the funded program (crime reduction, diversions, bail compliance, dollars returned) - the initiative was selected Feb 2024 and no evaluation appears published yet; named local coordinators/chairs of the CINC justice reinvestment initiative (only 'Karen', a volunteer, is named on the CINC site, and the ANU researcher Dr Jill Guthrie); the original publication year of the ANU community report (page marked 'Completed' with no date). The Cowra Shire Council media release (cowracouncil.com.au) returned HTTP 403 and the AG.gov page timed out, so council resolution dates/details are taken second-hand from the ANU report summary, not directly verified.

The ANU report records that Cowra Shire Council passed a resolution supporting Justice Reinvestment and committing to advocate for a JR pilot in Cowra (date not verified). The research reference group included Cowra Aboriginal Land Council, Cowra Shire Council, and local and international experts.

CULTURAL NOTE: This is a First Nations justice reinvestment initiative; the impact figures above are research-participation counts and program-wide funding only, not community outcomes - do not present them as crime/incarceration results. No deceased-person or naming-safety flags identified.

sites.notes: CONFIRMED WEBSITE CHOICE: The site's given website (justicereinvestment.net.au) is the NATIONAL Justice Reinvestment Network Australia index page, not Nowra-specific. The real site page is the Just Reinvest NSW community page for Nowra (justreinvest.org.au/community/nowra/) — used as confirmed_website. The Nowra work is operationally LED BY WAMINDA, so the logo is Waminda's (taken directly from the Nowra page's ); there is no separate "Nowra Justice Reinvestment" logo. CRITICAL DATA-INTEGRITY FLAG ON IMPACT METRICS: As of the public record (June 2026) there are NO published JR-SITE-SPECIFIC outcome metrics for Nowra (no "crime down X%", bail compliance, diversions, or $ returned figures). This is an early-stage site (2023–2027 contract); 2023/24 milestones were process outputs (Program Logic / Theory of Change finalised, local solutions developed). The percentage-reduction and sittings figures I recorded are for CIRCLE SENTENCING — a related but DISTINCT NSW-wide program that was historically piloted in Nowra in 2002. I have labelled every such metric explicitly as "NOT a JR-site outcome" so they are not misread as Nowra JR impact. Do not present them as Justice Reinvestment results. There is no Maranguka-style independent impact evaluation for Nowra. FUNDING FIGURE DISCREPANCY: DCJ pages state the Nowra+Kempsey contract is "$2.5 million ... from June 2023 to June 2027", while a NSW Parliament answer (Q1204) describes the contract as "three years from June 2023 until June 2026." Both dates appear in official sources; flagged rather than resolved. CIRCLE SENTENCING SITTINGS DISCREPANCY: Ulladulla Times reports "more than 1,450" cumulative sittings; the DCJ media release states "over 1,518". Both quoted. PEOPLE / CULTURAL SENSITIVITY: I could NOT find a publicly named "Nowra Justice Reinvestment Coordinator/Manager" by name — public sources describe the Waminda JR team structurally ("two co-managers, a data officer, two caseworkers, a project support worker") without naming individuals. The named people are Waminda's Chief Executive Leadership Team (collective/shared leadership model, no individual titles published in the source); Hayley Longbottom is the most consistently public-facing as Chief Executive Leader. No named individuals were sourced from LinkedIn/ZoomInfo (excluded as non-primary / privacy). No cultural-sensitivity (deceased-person) flags identified, but recommend community sign-off on any named-leader display given the shared-leadership and Aboriginal-women-led nature of the org. NOT FOUND: dedicated stand-alone Nowra website/domain; Nowra-specific evaluation report; specific named JR coordinator; quantitative Nowra outcome data.

It is delivered under the broader Ngurrajuta Pmara/Ntjarra Local Decision Making agreement. 3. I returned the site's apple-touch-icon (https://ngur.com.au/wp-content/themes/ngurratjuta/apple-touch-icon-precomposed.png) as the closest real brand mark from the org's own site; a favicon.ico also exists at the same theme path. UNVERIFIED that the apple-touch-icon renders as the full logo — treat as best-available, not confirmed logo. 5. PEOPLE — CULTURAL-SENSITIVITY FLAG: I deliberately left people EMPTY. The org's history page names a 1985 FOUNDING committee (chair/secretary Alison Anderson plus ten founding members) but those are 40-year-old historical roles, not current JR leaders, and naming long-past committee members as program leaders would be misleading. The current board is published ONLY as an image file (https://ngur.com.au/wp-content/uploads/sites/16/Board-Members-as-at-26.11.2025.png) which I could not read as text, so I cannot verify current names. CONFIRMED FACTS: Nja-marléya Cultural Leaders and Justice Group Ltd (ABN 59 661 706 430, ACN 661 706 430), based on Kunibidji Country, Maningrida NT 0822, is an ACNC-registered charity. It was selected by an independent panel and funded in February 2024 under the Commonwealth National Justice Reinvestment program to deliver its Cultural Justice Model. Its model includes Senior Lore Elders/cultural leaders, the Bush Council early-intervention for youth, Cultural Plans via Jaga mentors, the Jaga cultural-mentoring program, and coordination with Police, Corrections, Territory Families and Health. It runs Community Court work under the NT Aboriginal Justice Agreement (2021-2027), preparing Aboriginal Experience Reports and participating in sentencing. sites.one_line: A First Nations-led justice reinvestment program on the Groote Archipelago (NT) that combines the Northern Territory's first legislated Community Justice Group, a Community Court, the Anindilyakwa Peacemaker Program and the Anindilyakwa Healing Centre, with Anindilyakwa Royalties Aboriginal Corporation as the federally selected funding recipient.

history.event: Anindilyakwa Land Council officially launched the Community Justice Group, comprised of community Elders and respected persons supporting peacemaking and offending-reduction initiatives.

ARAC selected as one of nine organisations to deliver Commonwealth justice reinvestment initiatives, announced 1 February 2024 by Minister for Indigenous Australians Linda Burney as part of a $79M / $109M First Nations justice package.

Landmark alternative justice process announced (3 Feb 2024): NT's first legislated community justice group and re-established Community Court on Groote Eylandt, backed by $13M NT Government investment over four years.
Groote Archipelago Community Justice Group program established (November 2024) as the NT's first legislated community justice group.
Anindilyakwa Healing Centre (32-bed residential rehabilitation for young men, operated by DASA) completed March 2025.
Reported 87.51% fall in total offences (130 vs 1,041 in 2019) and major youth-crime decline since the Community Court and Community Justice Group were established.

programs: Groote Archipelago Community Justice Group (NT's first legislated community justice group)
Community Court (re-established on Groote Eylandt)
Anindilyakwa Peacemaker Program
Anindilyakwa Healing Centre (32-bed residential rehabilitation for young men 17-25)
Youth mentoring and practical support (cooking, fitness, driving lessons)

sites.notes:
ORG-OF-RECORD vs DELIVERY: The funding recipient named in the federal program is Anindilyakwa Royalties Aboriginal Corporation (ARAC), but the justice work is delivered through the Anindilyakwa Land Council (ALC) and the Groote Archipelago Community Justice Group. ARAC itself has a separate website focused on the Township Lease (aracleasing.com.au) with no justice-program content; the ALC site carries the program (Peacemaker Program page used as confirmed_website). The generic homepage anindilyakwa.com.au has no justice-reinvestment content.

LOGO: logo_url is the real ALC logo from the ALC site HTML (wp-content/uploads/2020/12/alc-logo.png). No separate ARAC or program-specific logo was found.

FIGURE CAUTION: Multiple distinct crime-reduction figures circulate for different periods - do not conflate. (1) 95% youth-crime decline is 2018-19 (346) to 2021-22 (17 offences); (2) 87.51% total-offence fall is "past 12 months" (130) vs 2019 (1,041) per the July 2025 NIT report. The NIT headline rounds 87.51% to '88 per cent'. The $79M/$109M figures are the NATIONAL program totals, not Groote-specific. The $6.2M is specifically the Peacemaker Program (NIAA). The $13M/$11M/$1.12M are NT Government figures from the Feb 2024 NIT article.

COULD NOT FIND: A single official, ARAC-published per-site funding figure for the Commonwealth justice reinvestment grant; a formal published evaluation report PDF (figures are quoted via NIT/Smarter Justice/NIAA rather than a downloadable evaluation). Croakey article (root-causes-of-crime) returned HTTP 403 and could not be read. AG.gov.au justice reinvestment index timed out.

CULTURAL SENSITIVITY: One Peacemaker on the NIAA page is referred to only by initials 'LW' (a Warnumamalya man) - retained as initials only, not expanded. Tony Wurramarrba and Cherelle Wurrawilya are both reported as ALC Chair in different-dated sources (2024 vs 2025), suggesting a leadership change; both retained with their source dates. No deceased-person naming flags identified, but verify locally before any public republication of personal names per Anindilyakwa protocols.

sites.one_line: The Doomadgee Justice Reinvestment program, run by the community-owned organisation Gunawuna Jungai Limited, is a federally-funded community-led initiative to reduce Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander contact with the justice system in Doomadgee, north-west Queensland.

history.event: Doomadgee became the first Queensland site nominated for a Place-Based Partnership under Closing the Gap, with Gunawuna Jungai selected as one of six locations nationwide.
Gunawuna Jungai awarded federal Justice Reinvestment funding as one of 10 organisations under the Commonwealth's $109 million First Nations justice package; announcement reported 26 April 2024.
Gunawuna Jungai secured Justice Reinvestment pilot/site status and developed a work plan to reduce youth interaction with the justice system through community-based prevention and early intervention.
Initial Justice Reinvestment Steering Committee gathering held in December 2024; Terms of Reference developed.

Terms of Reference approved (January 2025) and Activity Work Plan finalised with the Attorney-General's Department; first Activity Work Plan Report delivered February 2025; Steering Committee expanded; board refresh and 'Justice Our Way' Summit attendance March/April 2025.

sites.notes:
This is an EARLY-STAGE program. Federal Justice Reinvestment funding was announced in April 2024 and 2025-26 has been a foundational/planning phase (Steering Committee, Terms of Reference, Activity Work Plan, community engagement). As a result there are NO crime-reduction, bail-compliance, diversion, or recidivism outcome metrics in the public record yet — nothing comparable to the 2018 KPMG Maranguka report. The impact_metrics returned are foundational/output figures and funding-package context only.

COULD NOT FIND: (1) the specific dollar amount allocated to Gunawuna Jungai/Doomadgee — every source only gives the national $109M package and $79M/30-sites figure, not the per-site grant. (2) Any independent evaluation or impact report. (3) Quantified crime, incarceration, or youth-diversion baselines/outcomes for Doomadgee.

DATA NOTES: The 'first full year' framing for the 29-ABNs figure and the population/FIFO figures come from the Tim Fairfax Family Foundation profile, which spans Gunawuna Jungai's broader Closing the Gap place-based work, not strictly the JR program; the 29-ABN figure is plausibly attributable to the economic-development arm that the JR jobs strategy feeds into rather than JR outcomes per se. The 2026-priorities PDF on the org site did not parse as readable text (binary/encoded), so its contents could not be verified.

LOGO: https://jungai.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/GWJ-Logo.png was VERIFIED directly via curl (HTTP 200, image/png) even though one WebFetch attempt returned a transient 404. This is the org's real logo on its own domain.

CULTURAL SENSITIVITY: Named people are all current, publicly-listed program leaders/staff/Traditional Owners quoted in media or on the org's own site — no apparent deceased-person or naming-safety concerns. Gangalidda and Waanyi peoples; 'Gunawuna' = 'child/every child/everyone', 'Jungai' = 'authority/boss' in Gangalidda language. Steering-committee member names listed on the org page were not all individually re-verified against a second source.

sites.one_line: Cherbourg Wellbeing Indigenous Corporation, based in Cherbourg, Queensland, is one of nine First Nations organisations selected in February 2024 to deliver a community-led justice reinvestment initiative under the Albanese Government's federal package.

history.event: Justice reinvestment project initiated in Cherbourg in April 2017 by the Queensland Department of Justice and Attorney-General (Youth Justice) — described as the first attempt to implement all four formal stages of justice reinvestment in Queensland, targeting incarceration rates of young Indigenous people aged 10-25.

Queensland DJAG and Youth Justice consulted community members, organisations and stakeholders in Cherbourg on whether and how justice reinvestment could be implemented locally, via interviews, surveys and community forums; Cherbourg Local Council endorsed the consultant's final report.

Cherbourg Wellbeing Indigenous Corporation named as one of nine organisations selected to deliver justice reinvestment initiatives under the Albanese Government's First Nations justice package (announced 1-2 February 2024).

sites.notes:
CONFIRMATION STATUS: Cherbourg Wellbeing Indigenous Corporation is a real entity — confirmed by the federal funding announcement (Mirage News, naming it among nine funded orgs) and a Cherbourg Aboriginal Shire Council Facebook post advertising its support-worker positions. It was selected in Feb 2024 to lead a community-led justice reinvestment initiative.

COULD NOT FIND: (1) A dedicated program/organisation website — confirmed_website is null; the supplied URL is the generic federal Attorney-General's index page, not a site for this program. (2) A real logo on any program-owned site — logo_url is null (none invented). (3) Any publicly-named JR coordinator, chairperson or backbone-team lead for THIS corporation that I could verify — people is empty rather than guessed. (4) The corporation's ICN/ORIC number. (5) Any published impact/outcome evaluation of the Cherbourg JR initiative — the program is early-stage (federal funding only from 2024).

DATA-PROVENANCE CAVEATS: The two impact_metrics ($1.5M annual youth incarceration cost; ~three-quarters community support) are 2017 consultation-stage figures from the Queensland Youth Justice JR-recommendations page. That page returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch, so the exact quotes were surfaced consistently across two independent web-search result summaries rather than read off the page directly — treat as moderate-confidence, verify against the live page before publishing. These are baseline/cost figures, NOT outcome figures.

IMPORTANT — DO NOT CONFLATE: The widely-cited 2018 KPMG Maranguka impact figures (23% drop in domestic violence, 31% Year-12 retention rise, 38% reduction in juvenile charges, $3.1M economic impact) belong to BOURKE, NSW — not Cherbourg. They appeared in search results only because Maranguka is the genre's canonical example. No equivalent evaluation exists for Cherbourg; none attributed here.

CULTURAL NOTE: No individuals named, partly because none could be verified and partly to avoid unsafe naming for an Indigenous community organisation without a confirmed public source. The 2011-2016 QPS recorded-offences dataset on ResearchGate (academic baseline, not a program evaluation) was deliberately excluded as it predates the initiative and is not an impact measure.

sites.one_line: Gindaja Treatment and Healing Indigenous Corporation, an Aboriginal community-controlled organisation in Yarrabah QLD, auspices the Yarrabah Justice Service and is one of nine organisations selected in 2024 to deliver a Commonwealth-funded justice reinvestment initiative.

history.event: The Yarrabah Community Justice Group (operating for roughly 30 years, originally under Yarrabah Council, then auspiced by Gurriny) was transferred to Gindaja as auspice organisation.

In June 2020 the Queensland Department of Justice and Attorney-General restructured the funding format; Gindaja tendered for and won the contract for the Yarrabah Justice Service, and a new service-delivery model was developed splitting the single coordinator role into a Coordinator role and a grassroots Justice Group Officer role.

On 1 February 2024 the Australian Government announced that, following an independent assessment, nine organisations were selected to deliver justice reinvestment initiatives, including Gindaja Treatment and Healing Indigenous Corporation (Yarrabah, QLD), under a $79 million commitment to support up to 30 community-led initiatives (part of a $109 million First Nations justice package).

Throughout 2025 the interim National Justice Reinvestment Unit engaged with Commonwealth-funded initiatives (including Gindaja) on the design of the National Justice Reinvestment Unit, holding four roundtables in Perth, Adelaide, Darwin and Cairns across July and August 2025; the first evaluation applying the national justice reinvestment framework is anticipated to begin later in 2026.

sites.notes:
NO Yarrabah-specific justice reinvestment impact metrics exist in the public record yet, so impact_metrics is intentionally empty rather than padded. The Gindaja JR initiative was only selected for Commonwealth funding on 1 Feb 2024, and per the Attorney-General's Department the first evaluation under the national justice reinvestment framework is not anticipated to begin until later in 2026. The widely-cited Maranguka (Bourke) KPMG-style outcome figures do NOT apply here. Organisation-level operational figures appear on Gindaja's site / QAIHC article (17 residential-recovery beds, 10 Step Up accommodation beds, ~80 residential clients/year, ~175 day-program clients, 50+ mostly local Yarri staff) but these describe Gindaja's alcohol-and-other-drug treatment service, NOT justice reinvestment outcomes, so they are deliberately excluded from impact_metrics to avoid misattribution.

The $79M / "up to 30 initiatives" / 9-orgs-selected figures are NATIONAL program figures, not Gindaja's individual allocation. Gindaja's specific dollar allocation was not found in the public sources checked.

confirmed_website is Gindaja's own dedicated Justice Services page (gindaja.org.au/justice-services/), not the generic AG index page given as the input website. logo_url verified to return HTTP 200 / image/webp (it is the org's main Gindaja logo; no separate Justice Service logo exists). 

People: Ailsa Lively is named as Gindaja's CEO via a QAIHC Sector Leader article reference; she leads the whole corporation, not specifically the Justice Service. The Justice Service's individual Coordinator and Justice Group Officer are described as roles but no current post-holders are publicly named — not naming them avoids potential cultural-safety / privacy issues. Gindaja's board-of-directors page exists but lists no individual names in the accessible content. No deceased persons were named. Standard cultural-sensitivity caution applies: Yarrabah is a discrete Aboriginal community and naming should be checked with the community before publication.

Confidence: history/funding/partners are Verified against primary sources (Gindaja site + AG/Ministers' media centre). The CEO name is Inferred from a secondary article reference. Absence of impact metrics is Verified (AG states evaluation begins later 2026).

sites.one_line: Jika Kangka Gununamanda Limited is the community-controlled backbone organisation running Thaldii Karrwa ('Stand up strong'), the justice reinvestment initiative on Mornington Island, Queensland, uniting the Lardil, Kaiadilt and Yankaal peoples.

history.event: The Mornington Island Restorative Justice (MIRJ) Project — an earlier community-led peacemaking/mediation program on the same island, delivered by Junkuri Laka — was independently evaluated (Colmar Brunton, National Data Collection, Social Policy and Evaluation Research).
The Mornington Island Restorative Justice Project (delivered by Junkuri Laka) was recognised as the national winner of the Australian Crime and Violence Prevention Award.

On 19 April 2024 the Commonwealth announced Mornington Island (Jika Kangka Gununamanda Limited) among ten new community-led justice reinvestment initiatives, part of a national package funding up to 30 sites.

programs: Thaldii Karrwa ('Stand up strong') — the Mornington Island justice reinvestment initiative
Circuit breaker programs focused on cultural, social and emotional wellbeing to prevent contact with the justice system
Community leadership development
Elder and youth-led initiatives for First Nations teenagers (reducing social isolation, increasing engagement with education and employment)

sites.notes:
CRITICAL DISTINCTION FOR THIS PLATFORM: There are two related-but-distinct programs on Mornington Island. (1) The current Justice Reinvestment initiative is 'Thaldii Karrwa' ('Stand up strong'), with Jika Kangka Gununamanda Limited (JKG) as the community-controlled backbone organisation, announced 19 April 2024 under the Commonwealth's national JR package ($79M for up to 30 sites; QLD has committed $5M over 3 years). NO published evaluation or impact figures exist yet for Thaldii Karrwa — it is new (2024). (2) The strong impact metrics in this record (10.6 benefit-cost ratio, $17.35M NPV, 90% community peace perception, 160 mediations/year, 2015 national award) ALL belong to the EARLIER 'Mornington Island Restorative Justice Project' (MIRJ), delivered by Junkuri Laka Wellesley Islands Aboriginal Law, Justice and Governance Association Inc., evaluated 2014 (Colmar Brunton) with a cost-benefit analysis directed by Prof. Anne Daly (Univ. of Canberra). These are flagged inline in each impact_metric. Do NOT present MIRJ/Junkuri Laka figures as Thaldii Karrwa JR outcomes.

NOT FOUND: No named JKG leaders, directors, chair, or coordinator could be publicly confirmed — the org's About page (jkg.org.au/about) is an unpopulated CMS placeholder, and no media release named a community spokesperson. people[] left empty per the no-invention rule. No per-site Commonwealth funding figure for Mornington Island was disclosed in any source. The AG justice reinvestment page (the provided 'website') is a generic Commonwealth index, not site-specific; the real homepage is jkg.org.au.

CULTURAL NOTE: JKG unites three peoples — Lardil, Kaiadilt and Yankaal. No deceased persons were named in any source. A Facebook page exists (facebook.com/jikakangka) but was not used as a primary source. Logo URL was extracted from the live JKG homepage HTML (a real hosted image), not generated.

sites.one_line: Napranum Aboriginal Shire Council in Napranum, Queensland was selected in September 2024 as one of six new community-led justice reinvestment initiatives funded under the Australian Government's National Justice Reinvestment Program.

history.event: Napranum Aboriginal Shire Council (Napranum, QLD) selected as one of six new community-led justice reinvestment initiatives, announced by Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus and Minister for Indigenous Australians Malarndirri McCarthy on 30 September 2024, following assessment by an independent panel. Part of the Government's $79 million commitment to support up to 30 community-led initiatives nationally.

sites.notes:
VERIFIED: Napranum Aboriginal Shire Council (Napranum, QLD) was named on 30 Sep 2024 as one of six new community-led justice reinvestment initiatives under the Australian Government National Justice Reinvestment Program, announced by AG Mark Dreyfus and Minister Malarndirri McCarthy (source: ministers.pmc.gov.au + ministers.ag.gov.au). The broader package is $79M for up to 30 initiatives nationally (NIT). Canonical JR definition (verbatim, from the ministerial release): "The national justice reinvestment program is a long-term, community-led approach to preventing crime, improving community safety and reducing the number of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people in contact with the justice system."

COULD NOT FIND (and did not invent): (1) NO per-initiative funding figure for Napranum is published in any source I could reach — GrantConnect (grants.gov.au), which would hold the awarded value, returned HTTP 403 and was not readable; (2) NO impact_metrics exist in the public record — this is a newly funded initiative (late 2024 onward) with no published evaluation, crime/diversion/bail statistics, or dollar-returned figures. The 2018 KPMG Maranguka genre of impact data does NOT yet exist for Napranum. Returning an empty impact_metrics array rather than padding; (3) NO dedicated program website — the council homepage (napranum.qld.gov.au) is a generic local-government site and does not mention justice reinvestment at all, so confirmed_website is null; (4) NO publicly named justice reinvestment coordinator/program lead found. The council's own Elected Representatives page names Mayor Cr Roy Chevathen; I list him as the council leader, NOT as a confirmed JR program lead.

DATA CONFLICT (flagged): A web-search snippet aggregation claimed the Mayor is "Janita Motton" and CEO "Peter O'May". I did NOT use these — the council's own elected-representatives page (primary source) names Mayor Cr Roy Chevathen, Deputy Mayor Cr Ernest Madua, and Crs Margie Adidi, Egito Mairu, Robert Wigness (all elected/serving 2024). I trusted the primary source over the snippet. CEO name was only seen in an unverified search snippet, so it is omitted.

CULTURAL NOTE: Napranum (meaning 'meeting place') is a remote Aboriginal community on western Cape York. Initiative is First-Nations-led; treat naming of individuals conservatively. No deceased-person naming concerns identified.

LOGO: verified present on the council site header (wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Logo-14.png); it is the Napranum Aboriginal Shire Council logo, not a dedicated JR-program logo.

Not fetched due to size limits: council Annual Reports 2023-24 and 2024-25 PDFs and the Oct/Apr 2025 NASC newsletters (all >10MB or exceeded fetch content limit) — these may contain Napranum-specific JR program detail and named staff and are the best next sources if a deeper pass is run via a PDF-capable tool.

sites.one_line: Tiraapendi Wodli ("protecting home" in Kaurna) is a community-led Aboriginal justice reinvestment initiative in Port Adelaide, South Australia, run in partnership with the local Aboriginal Leadership Group, Australian Red Cross and Justice Reinvestment SA.

history.event: Port Adelaide Aboriginal Leadership Group formed (March 2018) to begin visioning a justice reinvestment approach; project began in earnest in 2018 as a collaboration between the Tiraapendi Wodli Aboriginal leadership group, the local Aboriginal community, Australian Red Cross and Justice Reinvestment SA.
Inaugural Tiraapendi Wodli Action Plan 2019-21 released (December 2018), focusing on four populations: primary school children, young people, men and families.

Released a Theory of Change framework; in October 2023 Red Cross, Tiraapendi Wodli and Justice Reinvestment SA formed a partnership with the Paul Ramsay Foundation to implement the Understanding, Monitoring, Evaluation and Learning (UMEL) framework (2023-26).
Community evaluators began implementation of the evaluation with Associate Professors Alwin Chong and Fiona Arney and Dr Martine Hawkes (early 2024).
Tiraapendi Wodli formally registered as Tiraapendi Wodli Ltd on 4 April 2025, working towards independent operations.

- The "73 children" figure is the headline impact metric. It is reproduced on the org's own About JR page as a Paul Ramsay Foundation estimate ("PRF estimates the partners prevented 73 children and young people from going back to detention"). It is a PRF estimate across partners, not an independently published evaluation result. The community-led UMEL evaluation (2023-26, Chong/Arney/Hawkes) was still in progress as of 2024-25; no final published evaluation report or hard outcome figures (recidivism %, bail compliance, dollars returned) were found in the public record.
- The other figures (50% prison growth, $150M/yr, 23x disparity, $1M/child, Texas $241M/$444M) are contextual statistics the org cites to frame the justice-reinvestment case, NOT measured outcomes of this program.
- COULD NOT VERIFY a current board/director list. A WebFetch summary returned named directors with ASIC "Director ID" numbers (Edward Martin, Neva Wilson, Alicia Papertalk, Samantha Jackson) — those Director ID numbers are almost certainly fabricated (director IDs are never published on org sites), so I excluded all of them rather than risk inventing people. Only Deb Moyle (Lead/CEO, confirmed by PRF page + indigenousjustice.gov.au PDF) and Syd Sparrow (inaugural Chair, from the org site) are included with confidence. The three named academics are evaluators, not org leaders.
- justicereinvestmentsa.org/projectssa would not load (ECONNREFUSED) — JRSA is a confirmed delivery partner but I could not pull its project page directly.
- Logo URL verified live: returns HTTP 200, Content-Type image/jpeg (Squarespace CDN header image).

This is a recently-funded establishment-stage justice reinvestment initiative (Commonwealth support secured ~April 2024), not a mature program like Maranguka/Bourke. As a result there are NO program-specific impact metrics in the public record: no crime-reduction percentages, diversion counts, bail-compliance figures, or dollars-returned. impact_metrics is deliberately empty rather than padded with guesses.

FUNDING FIGURE NOT FOUND at the site level. Public sources confirm NRA is one of ~10 initiatives in the 2024 round under the $109M First Nations justice package ($79M for up to 30 JR initiatives), but the specific grant amount awarded to Ngarrindjeri Regional Authority Inc could not be retrieved — GrantConnect (GO6670) returned 403 and the AG.gov.au page timed out. The per-site dollar value would likely be on grants.gov.au / communitygrants.gov.au but is not accessible via web fetch here.

PEOPLE / CULTURAL NOTE: Grant Rigney (Chairperson) and Jaimie Pearson (General Manager – Justice Reinvestment) are publicly named in their professional capacities (gov PDF / public LinkedIn). The org's own JR page does NOT name individuals (it refers to the NRA Board, Research/Policy/Planning Unit, and Project Team by role) — possibly intentional. Both named people are living public office-holders; no deceased persons named. ZoomInfo was used only to corroborate role titles, not for contact details.

CONTEXT NOTE: The KNYA itself originated as a 2009 consultation/negotiation agreement between the SA Government and the NRA focused largely on natural-resource management and cultural authority following the Kumarangk (Hindmarsh Island) Royal Commission; justice reinvestment is a newer initiative operating under that KNYA umbrella. Do not conflate KNYA's broader history with the JR program's start date.

sites.one_line: The Ceduna / Far West SA justice reinvestment effort is an Australian Red Cross-led community-engagement initiative ("Justice Redesign in Ceduna and Far West, SA"), linked to the Justice Reinvestment SA network, working with the Ceduna, Yalata, Oak Valley and Koonibba communities to build a community-owned approach to reducing offending and incarceration.

history.event: Australian Red Cross commenced community engagement on justice issues with Aboriginal communities in and around Ceduna (engagement reported as commencing February 2015), funded by the Ian Potter Foundation and Collier Charitable Fund and linked to the South Australian Justice Reinvestment Working Party.

First stage of the Red Cross-supported justice reinvestment trial enabled the formation of relationships with the Ceduna, Yalata, Oak Valley and Koonibba communities and the 10 Aboriginal Homelands through the Far West Mojo program (Collier Charitable Fund supporting key components).
Red Cross recruiting a Community Projects Coordinator - Justice based in Ceduna to support the justice reinvestment / justice redesign work in the Far West region.

Khatija Thomas and Ralph Bonig appear in search as JRSA co-coordinators at the STATE-NETWORK level, NOT as named Ceduna site leaders; treat their source_url (aboutjr) as inferred — I could not directly load JRSA pages to confirm (see below). IMPACT METRICS: NONE found. This Ceduna initiative is at the engagement/consultation stage (building a community-owned justice action plan); the public record describes activities and relationships, not measured outcomes. There is NO Maranguka-style evaluation report with crime-down / bail-compliance / dollars-returned figures for Ceduna. I deliberately returned an empty impact_metrics array rather than borrow Maranguka or national-program figures. The only quantitative figure available is the 2016 Census Far West population of 3,321 (Platform C), which is demographic context, not an impact metric, so it is excluded.

COULD NOT VERIFY / ACCESS LIMITS: The JRSA domain (justicereinvestmentsa.org, a Wix site) refused all programmatic fetches from this environment (ECONNREFUSED / HTTP 000), and web.archive.org and the Collier PDF were also blocked/timed out, so I could NOT inspect the JRSA homepage HTML for a logo or og:image. logo_url is therefore null by rule (no guessing). The /aboutjr and /projectssa URLs are listed as sources_checked because they are the canonical JRSA pages, but their content here is drawn from search-engine snippets, not a direct page load — treat people[] and the confirmed_website's exact content as inferred from snippets rather than directly verified.

DATA QUALITY: moderate — partner/community structure and funders are well-attested across multiple sources; named site-level leadership and any impact figures are the gaps. Dates: the February 2015 start is from a search snippet attributed to AG/Red Cross material; year-level confidence is good, exact-month is inferred. The Far West Aboriginal corporations (Ceduna, Koonibba, Yalata, Scotdesco) and the Far West Aboriginal Communities Leaders Group are the Indigenous-governance bodies; any public listing of individual Chairpersons/CEOs should be confirmed against those corporations' own sites before naming, which I did not do here out of cultural-sensitivity caution.

sites.one_line: Kinchela Boys Home Aboriginal Corporation (KBHAC), a survivor-led Aboriginal community-controlled organisation in Sydney, runs the first Stolen Generations "Collective Justice Reinvestment" project and is one of ten community-led justice reinvestment sites backed by the Commonwealth's First Nations Justice Package.

history.event: Kinchela Aboriginal Boys Training Home opens; operates as a NSW government-run institution forcibly removing Aboriginal boys from their families.
The home closes in May 1970, with the last boys transferred out that month, after over 50 years of operation (1924-1970).
KBH survivors hold a reunion, 'K.B.H. The Journey Home', a milestone in the survivor-led movement that became KBHAC.
Tiffany McComsey appointed CEO of KBHAC by the board; she has been involved in the NSW Justice Reinvestment for Aboriginal Young People campaign.
KBHAC receives $390,000 through NSW Health's Stolen Generations Organisations Health Care Coordinator Grants program.
KBHAC (Sydney, NSW) named one of the first ten community-led justice reinvestment initiatives to secure Commonwealth support under the First Nations Justice Package (announced 19 April 2024).
Centenary (100th) commemoration of the Kinchela Aboriginal Boys Home; KBHAC calls for the site to become a national centre of truth-telling and healing.

sites.notes:
CULTURAL SENSITIVITY: This is a Stolen Generations survivor-led organisation dealing with forced child removal, intergenerational trauma, deaths in institutional care, and possible unmarked graves at the former Kinchela site. Any map presentation should foreground KBHAC's own framing ('Unlocking our past to free our future', survivor-led, truth-telling) and avoid sensational language. The number of children removed is given as a range (400-600) by the org itself.

WHAT IS DISTINCTIVE: Kinchela's 'justice reinvestment' is not the conventional youth-diversion / crime-reduction model (e.g. Maranguka in Bourke). It is framed by KBHAC as 'the first Stolen Generations collective justice reinvestment project' — a truth-telling and data/evidence-gathering project documenting the ongoing impact of Stolen Generations policies and the over-representation of survivors and descendants in child protection and justice systems. It shifts decision-making to the survivor community.

WHAT COULD NOT BE FOUND: No quantified justice-reinvestment outcome metrics yet (no crime-down %, bail-compliance, young-people-diverted, or cost-savings figures) — the project is early-stage and data-gathering, so there is no published evaluation comparable to the 2018 KPMG Maranguka report. The exact per-site Commonwealth dollar amount allocated specifically to Kinchela was NOT published (only the package-level $79M / up-to-30-sites figure). KBHAC's incorporation/establishment year was not stated on the pages reviewed. No JR program coordinator named beyond the CEO; board members not named on public pages reviewed (homepage links 'Our Board' but did not list individuals in the fetched content).

AMBIGUITY: The $390,000 (July 2023) is a NSW Health Stolen Generations health-coordinator grant, NOT justice-reinvestment funding — included for context but flagged as a separate program. The ag.gov.au justice-reinvestment index page timed out on fetch and could not be confirmed directly; the April 2024 funding is corroborated via National Indigenous Times. The confirmed_website points to the program-specific JR page; the org homepage is https://kinchelaboyshome.org.au/.

sites.notes:
CONFIRMED FACTS: "Derby Justice Reinvestment" is the local justice reinvestment initiative led by Emama Nguda Aboriginal Corporation (formed 1994, Derby WA). It was selected in Feb 2024 as one of nine first-round sites under the Australian Government's First Nations justice reinvestment program ($79M for up to 30 initiatives, within a $109M package). CEO is Ben Burton (publicly named, alive, quoted in his own voice on the org's site and in NIT - safe to name). The org's own "landmark-investment" page frames the JR funding as an expansion of existing programs (Night Patrol, basketball-at-night, meals, family support).

COULD NOT FIND: (1) No site-specific dollar figure for the Derby justice reinvestment grant - the federal release does not break funding down by organisation, and the org's site gives no Derby-specific JR figure. (2) No standalone justice-reinvestment program webpage on the org's site; the dedicated /funded-programs page lists programs but does not use the "justice reinvestment" label (Target 120 is the closest). So confirmed_website is the org homepage, not a JR-specific page (none exists). (3) No formal published evaluation/impact report in the genre of the 2018 KPMG Maranguka report. (4) No board chairperson publicly named.

METRIC CAVEATS (verification-critical): The "10-15% reduction in youth crime" and "500-700 young people weekly" figures come from a Dec 2024 National Indigenous Times feature and are attributed there to the Derby Youth Night Patrol (an Emama Nguda program that the JR funding expands), NOT to a standalone "justice reinvestment" evaluation - they are reported figures, not independently evaluated outcomes. The Target 120 "nearly half had no further police contact" figure is for the statewide WA Target 120 program (which Emama Nguda delivers in Derby), reported April 2023. The $79M and $43M figures are national/statewide program totals, not Derby allocations. Treat all metrics as as-reported, not evaluated.

CULTURAL: Ben Burton is named publicly and quoted in his own voice; naming is appropriate. No deceased persons referenced. Programs are explicitly Aboriginal-led ("Our staff are all local Aboriginal people").

verified_metrics.note: Page loaded and states verbatim: 'As part of the $109 million First Nations justice package, the Australian Government has committed $79 million to supporting up to 30 community-led justice reinvestment initiatives in First Nations communities across Australia.' Both the $79M/30-initiatives figure and the $109M package figure appear exactly as reported.

sites.one_line: Katherine Justice Reinvestment is a community-led initiative in Katherine, NT, delivered by a consortium of Savanna Solutions, Jesuit Social Services and Flinders University, working to address the over-representation of Aboriginal people in the justice system through prevention and early intervention.

history.event: Ms Fiona Allison (then James Cook University) worked alongside Katherine stakeholders on initial justice reinvestment consultations, the genesis of the work.

The Katherine Youth Justice Reinvestment Group (KYJRG) was established, with the aim of transforming youth justice services and systems in Katherine to focus on prevention and early intervention to reduce incarceration.

In December 2017, Australian Red Cross (acting on behalf of the KYJRG) contracted research relating to youth Justice Reinvestment in Katherine; the project's reporting period ran 1 December 2017 to 31 May 2019.

Menzies School of Health Research published the 'Katherine Youth Justice Reinvestment: Final Report' (Smith, Allison, Christie, Clifford, Robertson, Ireland & Wallace, June 2019), a baseline community-consultation study.
In April 2024 the K Town Justice Group secured funding to deliver the Justice Reinvestment project through a consortium of Savanna Solutions, Jesuit Social Services and Flinders University.
Flinders University co-delivered the 'Katherine Justice Reinvestment: Have Your Say' symposium on 19-20 September 2024 at the Godinymayin Yijard Rivers Arts and Culture Centre.

programs: Training and employment mentoring circuit-breaker initiative (delivered by Savanna Solutions)

IMPACT METRICS - IMPORTANT HONESTY FLAG: There are NO published quantitative OUTCOME metrics (e.g. crime down X%, bail compliance up, recidivism, young people diverted, $ returned) for Katherine JR in the public record. The only verifiable figures are (a) consultation-participation counts from the 2019 Menzies baseline study (a qualitative consultation, NOT an impact evaluation) and (b) symposium registration numbers. Do not present these as crime/justice outcomes - they are process/engagement figures. I have labelled them as such.

FUNDING AMOUNT: The specific April 2024 grant amount to the Katherine consortium is NOT disclosed publicly. The $10M-over-4-years federal commitment found in search relates to CENTRAL AUSTRALIA justice reinvestment, a DIFFERENT NT region - I deliberately did NOT attribute it to Katherine to avoid a false figure.

DATE DISCREPANCY: Secondary sources (Croakey/web) say the group 'formed in 2022, building on an initial group set up in 2015'. The primary 2019 Menzies report states KYJRG was 'established in 2016' and Fiona Allison's foundational work was '2015-2016'. PEOPLE/CULTURAL SENSITIVITY: No single named coordinator or chair of KJR/K Town Justice Group is publicly identified - governance is via the community group and consortium, not a named individual. The people listed are researchers (Allison, Smith) and named community symposium contributors (Christine Butler - Bandjin woman; Rick Fletcher) reported in Flinders' own news. I did NOT name any individuals as 'leaders' of the community group since the public record does not support that, and avoided naming any youth participants. sites.notes:
IDENTITY/DISAMBIGUATION FLAG (important): The record labels this site "Balga Justice Reinvestment / Ebenezer Aboriginal Corporation." Ebenezer Aboriginal Corporation (a service of the Balga Evangelical Aboriginal Church, providing youth accommodation support for 50+ years; office Unit 1, 3 Exhibition Dr, Malaga WA) IS confirmed as a federally-funded JR site (1 of 9 named in the 1 Feb 2024 Commonwealth announcement). Its own JR programs are named on its website: a Family JR stream, an Adult JR stream, and the youth "Kaarditjin Baldja - Justice Re-Investment in Schools." JR contact: justicereinvestment@ebenezer.org.au / 0402 981 998.

DO NOT CONFLATE WITH "OLD WAYS NEW WAYS": A separate, well-documented Perth metro JR program called "Old Ways New Ways" also operates in Balga (and Armadale). It is led by the Aboriginal Legal Service of WA (ALSWA) with Wadjuk Northside Aboriginal Corporation, HOPE Community Services and the Stephen Michael Foundation — funded ~$3M/year federally to 30 June 2026. Across all sources checked, Ebenezer is NOT named as a partner in Old Ways New Ways, and Old Ways New Ways does not name Ebenezer. The two are distinct Balga JR initiatives. Old Ways New Ways' figures (282 children referred / 105 active / 24 waitlisted over ~13.5 months from an Oct-2024 launch, per NIT 12 Dec 2025) and its named staff (Bruce Loo, Jamie Jackson, Anna-Shae Michael, Liam Anthony; Lenny Yarran of Wadjak Northside; Victoria Williams, ALSWA Director Programs; Wayne Nannup, ALSWA CEO) belong to the ALSWA program — I have therefore NOT entered them as Ebenezer impact_metrics or people. If the map intends this entry to BE Old Ways New Ways, the org field is wrong (should be ALSWA) and a separate richer record exists.

COULD NOT FIND for Ebenezer's own JR work specifically: any published impact metrics/outcomes; named coordinators/chairperson/CEO with a source; named delivery partners or specific funding amount; a launch date for the Ebenezer JR program; a dedicated JR landing page (its homepage only lists the service streams). impact_metrics is therefore empty — none could be sourced to Ebenezer without misattributing the ALSWA program's numbers.

WEBSITE: confirmed_website set to https://ebenezer.org.au/ (the org's real homepage). sites.one_line: The Justice Reinvestment Carnarvon Common Ground Project is an Aboriginal-led, place-based justice reinvestment initiative in Carnarvon (Gascoyne region, WA), supported by the Gascoyne Development Commission and based around the Gwoonwardu Mia Cultural Centre.

history.event: Justice Reinvestment Carnarvon Common Ground Project commenced as a community-led initiative in response to crime and youth-related issues in the Gascoyne region, supported by the Gascoyne Development Commission.

Carnarvon (WA) selected as one of ten community-led justice reinvestment initiatives to secure Commonwealth support, following assessment by an independent panel, under the national $79 million / up to 30 initiatives program (announced April 2024).
Aboriginal-led community group secured initial Commonwealth funding of $1.4 million; negotiated workplan with the Commonwealth and secured staff and premises.
WA Governor His Excellency Chris Dawson and Mrs Darrilyn Dawson visited the project at Gwoonwardu Mia Cultural Centre, guided by Elder Bernie Ryder (Government House article, 26 March 2025).

A formal Commonwealth Government review early in 2026 found community engagement delivered at substantial scale and capacity-building activities implemented as planned; project secured further funding for at least the next 3 years.

sites.one_line: Old Ways New Ways is an Aboriginal-led justice reinvestment program led by the Aboriginal Legal Service of Western Australia, delivering culturally secure case management, mentoring and on-Country support to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander young people in the Perth metropolitan area (Balga and Armadale) who are at risk of or already involved in the criminal justice system.

history.event: Albanese Government announces $79 million for up to 30 community-led justice reinvestment initiatives (part of a $109 million First Nations justice package); the Wheatbelt and Perth, WA initiative led by Aboriginal Legal Service of Western Australia Limited is among the first 10 selected. Announced 19 April 2024 by Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus and Minister for Indigenous Australians Linda Burney.

ALSWA launches 'Old Ways New Ways' as a new chapter in justice reinvestment, funded at $3 million per year for two years from the Federal Government, delivered by a consortium of ALSWA, Wadjak Northside Aboriginal Corporation, HOPE Community Services and the Stephen Michael Foundation, working with high-risk young people through the Perth Children's Court.
Six-week pilot of Old Ways New Ways begins in October 2024.

Old Ways New Ways fully launches in February 2025, operating from two sites (Balga and Armadale) with separate weekly sessions for young males and females. Funded under the Federal National Justice Reinvestment Program through 30 June 2026.

sites.one_line: Mampu-Maninjaku is a community-led crime prevention, alcohol and drug counselling, and diversion program delivered by the Central Australian Youth Link-Up Service (CAYLUS), federally funded as the second initiative under the Justice Reinvestment in Central Australia Program, serving the communities of Nyirripi, Willowra, Yuendumu and Outstations.

history.event: The Australian Government opened applications for the Justice Reinvestment in Central Australia Program (the funding stream Mampu-Maninjaku was later selected under), backed by a $10 million over four years commitment for Central Australia announced in the 2023-24 Budget.

On 17 July 2025 the federal government announced that CAYLUS would deliver Mampu-Maninjaku, selected as the second initiative funded under the Central Australia Justice Reinvestment Program after an independent panel's assessment process.

National Indigenous Times reported (21 July 2025) the program would be delivered by CAYLUS in partnership with the Australian Childhood Foundation and the Southern Tanami Kurdiji Indigenous Corporation, serving Nyirripi, Willowra, Yuendumu and Outstations.

programs: Mampu-Maninjaku (community-led crime prevention, alcohol and drug counselling, and diversion program)

sites.notes:
IMPACT METRICS: None exist in the public record yet. The program was only funded/announced on 17 July 2025, so no evaluation, crime-reduction, or diversion outcome figures are available — this is a brand-new program, not a mature one like Maranguka. The figures available are FUNDING/COMMITMENT figures for the broader national program, not impact outcomes for Mampu-Maninjaku itself, so I deliberately did NOT list them as impact_metrics: $69 million over four years from 2022-23 nationally (up to 30 initiatives), $20 million per year ongoing from 2026-27, and $10 million over four years for Central Australia (2023-24 Budget). The CAYLUS homepage's "70% reduction in petrol sniffing / in excess of 90% in Central Australia (2009 Opal Fuel evaluation)" figure belongs to CAYLUS's petrol-sniffing work, NOT to Mampu-Maninjaku justice reinvestment, so I excluded it to avoid mis-attribution.

PEOPLE: No CAYLUS program coordinators, chairs, or community leaders for Mampu-Maninjaku are named in the public sources. The CAYLUS website lists two org-wide co-managers (Bianca Rayner, Business Manager; Liam Campbell, Operations Manager) but they are not identified as Mampu-Maninjaku leaders, so I did not list them under this specific program. Ministers Michelle Rowland (Attorney-General) and Senator Malarndirri McCarthy (Minister for Indigenous Australians) are the funders' representatives, not program leaders. Given this is an Indigenous-led, community-spearheaded program, naming individuals should be done from the community's own voice; none was published, so people is left empty.

WEBSITE: The CAYLUS homepage (caylus.org.au) does NOT mention Mampu-Maninjaku or justice reinvestment at all (checked 2026-06-13). There is no dedicated program website. confirmed_website is set to the CAYLUS org homepage as the closest real, working homepage for the delivering organisation. LinkedIn has a CAYLUS post about Mampu-Maninjaku but I did not treat a social post as a homepage.

NOT VERIFIED: The ag.gov.au ministers' page (a source_url) timed out twice and was not read directly; its content is corroborated by the identical PMC ministers' page which I did read. A 2026 reference to a "justice reinvestment initiative" appeared in a Redlands Coast (QLD) council search result — that is a DIFFERENT, unrelated initiative and not connected to Mampu-Maninjaku. CULTURAL FLAG: place names should appear with Indigenous names (Mparntwe/Alice Springs) per the program's own framing; no deceased-person or sensitive-naming issues encountered.

sites.one_line: An ACT Government justice reinvestment strategy (Phase 2: "RR25by25 and Beyond", launched September 2024) committing to reduce recidivism by 25% by 2025 and to reduce the over-representation of First Nations people in custody, delivered in partnership with First Nations community-controlled organisations including Yeddung Mura Aboriginal Corporation.

history.event: High Density Safety and Housing Project established under the 2004-2007 ACT Property Crime Strategy (delivery of participation activities to high and complex needs residents at seven public housing sites along Ainslie Ave).
ACT Government committed to developing an ACT Justice Reinvestment Strategy in the 2014-15 budget, aimed at reducing recidivism and diverting offenders.
Parliamentary Agreement for the 9th Legislative Assembly commitment to establish a Drug and Alcohol Court, as part of a goal to reduce recidivism by 25% by 2025.
Launch of justice reinvestment family trial (Yarrabi Bamirr) and announcement of the Ngurrambai Bail Support Trial in partnership with the Aboriginal Legal Service.
Announcement of the Building Communities, Not Prisons package as part of the 2019/20 Budget to expand justice reinvestment initiatives.
Commencement of the RR25by25 Plan (Reducing Recidivism in the ACT by 25% by 2025).
External reporting site at Yeddung Mura Aboriginal Corporation established to provide a culturally safe space for First Nations people on community-based sentences or parole orders.

Funding of a coordinated program of initiatives in partnership with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander organisations, including expansion of Yarrabi Bamirr and continuation of the Ngurrambai Bail Support Program.

Commencement of Phase 2 of the RR25by25 Plan - 'RR25by25 and Beyond: A Justice Reinvestment Strategy for the ACT' launched, with co-design of the Justice Futures Fund (foreword by AG Shane Rattenbury, September 2024).

sites.notes:
DATA QUALITY: rich. The primary source (the 36-page RR25by25 and Beyond strategy PDF, ACT Government, Sept 2024) is detailed and well-cited, yielding numerous exact-quote impact metrics with footnoted sources (ROGS, ANU, Australian Institute of Criminology).

WHAT THIS "SITE" IS: This is an ACT Government policy/strategy, not a standalone community organisation. "RR25by25" = "Reducing Recidivism in the ACT by 25% by 2025." Phase 2 (current, launched Sept 2024) is titled "RR25by25 and Beyond." Yeddung Mura (Good Pathways) Aboriginal Corporation is a key delivery partner (hosts the culturally-safe external reporting site since 2021 and runs a Throughcare program), but the strategy is government-owned and government-led, in partnership with the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Elected Body, the ATSI Justice Caucus and ACCOs.

LOGO: logo_url is the ACT Government inline-black logo found in the site header HTML and verified to resolve (HTTP 200, image/png). It is the correct REAL logo for this government-run site, NOT a Yeddung Mura logo. No og:image or distinct strategy/Yeddung Mura logo was found on the page. If the map prefers the delivery partner's mark, Yeddung Mura's own site would need to be checked separately (not done in this budget).

PEOPLE / CULTURAL SAFETY: Only two publicly-named, role-attributed leaders found: AG Shane Rattenbury MLA (strategy foreword author / announcing minister) and Dr Mindy Sotiri (ED, Justice Reform Initiative, third-party commentator). No First Nations individual leaders were named in the public sources reviewed in a way safe to attribute; the strategy references community bodies (Elected Body, Justice Caucus) rather than individuals. Skipped naming any First Nations individuals out of caution. NOTE: Shane Rattenbury was AG as of Sept 2024; the ACT held an election in Oct 2024 and the Attorney-General portfolio may since have changed (not verified in this research).

METRIC CAVEATS: The 19.6% recidivism decline is "since 2018-19" (Phase 1 progress toward the 25%-by-2025 target), not the final 2025 result. The "over one-quarter" First Nations prison share and "80%" overall reoffending figures come from the Justice Reform Initiative media release (advocacy NGO), not the government strategy. The Strong Connected Neighbourhoods and Drug & Alcohol court figures derive from named independent evaluations (Australian Institute of Criminology 2018; ANU 2022) cited within the strategy PDF.

NOT FOUND / GAPS: No formal published evaluation of "RR25by25 and Beyond" (Phase 2) itself (it only commenced 2024); evaluations exist for component programs. No total dollar value for the overall strategy (only the >$340k Justice Futures Fund co-design allocation). No Yeddung Mura-specific impact numbers beyond the qualitative review finding stakeholders "overwhelmingly supportive" of the reporting site.

sites.one_line: Yeddung Mura ("Good Pathways") is an Aboriginal-led Canberra corporation founded in 2013 that uses a justice reinvestment approach to support First Nations people leaving prison to reconnect with community, housing, and culture.

history.event: Canberra Elders came together over concern about services for First Nations people leaving prison and formed Yeddung Mura, starting with volunteers and a $1,000 donation from the local church.
Yeddung Mura registered as an Aboriginal corporation under the CATSI Act.
ACT Justice Housing Program (which includes Yeddung Mura's Transitional Accommodation Program for First Nations people) began assisting clients from May 2020.
ACTCS launched the Yeddung Mura External Reporting Site on 13 July 2021, providing a culturally safe community-corrections reporting site for eligible First Nations clients.
ACTCS published an evaluation of the Yeddung Mura External Reporting Site Pilot, finding clients, stakeholders and staff were overwhelmingly positive about its case-management and reporting outcomes.
Yeddung Mura launched Caring Dads in Canberra (described as an Australian-first), backed by $960,635 over two years in partnership with Kids First Australia.

programs: Your Journey, Our Support (reintegration program: Court Support, Prison Support, Community Support)
Transitional Accommodation Program (post-release housing for First Nations people, part of the ACT Justice Housing Program)
External Reporting Site (culturally safe community-corrections reporting site, launched 13 July 2021)

sites.notes:
CONFIDENCE / ATTRIBUTION CAVEATS:

LOGO: Verified directly via HTTP HEAD — https://goodpathways.org.au/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/Yeddung-Mura-logo1-re-1.png returns HTTP 200, content-type image/png, ~341KB. This is the real Yeddung Mura logo hosted on its own WordPress site.

JUSTICE REINVESTMENT FRAMING: Verified — Yeddung Mura is repeatedly described as a/the first ACT organisation built on a "justice reinvestment" model. The ORIC corporation story and the ACT Government Justice Housing Program release (quoting Attorney-General Shane Rattenbury) both use the term explicitly. Org's home is goodpathways.org.au; legal entity is Yeddung Mura Aboriginal Corporation, registered under the CATSI Act in 2014, founded 2013. Address: 56 Coyne Street, Fadden ACT 2904; phone 1800 864 663.

IMPORTANT METRIC ATTRIBUTION: The "188 clients", "10 houses", and "25% by 2025" figures come from the ACT Government's Justice Housing Program (JHP), a broader program in which Yeddung Mura's Transitional Accommodation Program for First Nations people is ONE component — these numbers are NOT solely Yeddung Mura's. I have labelled each metric accordingly. Do not present them as Yeddung-Mura-only outcomes. The release's named quotes (Emma Davidson MLA, Shane Rattenbury MLA, Bruno Aloisi Acting Commissioner ACTCS, Prof. Lorana Bartels ANU) relate to the JHP overall.

EVALUATION REPORT: The Yeddung Mura External Reporting Site Pilot Evaluation (ACT Corrective Services, 2023; PDF extracted and read directly) is QUALITATIVE — semi-structured interviews and case studies. It contains no hard outcome percentages/completion rates; its finding is that clients, stakeholders and staff were "overwhelmingly" positive. I did NOT manufacture a numeric outcome from it. It ties to the ACT's RR25By25 and the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Agreement 2019–2028.

SITE STATISTICS (incarceration rate 1,978.9 per 100,000; "21 times"; "nearly 100 adults"): these are AS-REPORTED ON YEDDUNG MURA'S OWN /act-justice-challenges PAGE, quoting an Australian Institute of Criminology "Reintegration of Indigenous Prisoners" source. The page does not give a precise ABS/ROGS citation for the 1,978.9 figure; treat as the org's self-reported context figures, not independently verified primary data.

PEOPLE / CULTURAL SAFETY: CEO Pastor Priestley Obed and Chairperson Deanne Booth are publicly named on the ORIC corporation story (a government-published profile). No indication of Sorry Business or naming sensitivity for these public leadership roles. The Yeddung Mura website itself did not surface staff names in the fetched content; names came from ORIC.

CARING DADS funding ($960,635 over two years, 2025) is from Region Canberra reporting; consistent with a DSS-listed family-violence activity. "Australian-first" is the outlet's framing.

DATA QUALITY: rich — own website (2 pages), a government corporation profile, an ACT Government media release with named quotes, a downloaded government evaluation PDF, and recent (2025) regional news all corroborate. Main gap: no Yeddung-Mura-specific quantified outcome metric exists in the public record (housing/recidivism numbers are JHP-wide; the ERS evaluation is qualitative).

Its existence, one-line description and the "23 times" stat were surfaced via the search index snippet of /aboutus and corroborated by the Tiraapendi Wodli /aboutjr page; treat the "23 times" quote as reported-via-search, not directly fetched from JRSA HTML.

LOGO: set to null. I could not inspect JRSA's own site HTML for its real logo/og:image due to the connection refusals. I found a valid REAL image on the related Tiraapendi Wodli site (https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/623ffc852c2f9a10b8073352/0ab8b4f7-b7c4-4c6b-95f1-ad059575d70a/Tiraapendi+Wodli+header.jpg — confirmed to return a real 86KB JPEG), but that is Tiraapendi Wodli's header, NOT the JRSA logo, so I did not use it as logo_url per the rule that it must be the org's own real logo.

PEOPLE / CULTURAL SAFETY: No JRSA-specific named leaders were found in the public record I could access (JRSA describes itself as a coalition with Red Cross coordinating). The five named people are publicly listed directors/officers/CEO of the partner entity Tiraapendi Wodli Ltd (from its public /abouttw page), explicitly labelled as such, not as JRSA staff. Several appear to be Aboriginal community leaders of a living organisation; I included only names publicly self-published on their own org site. No Sorry Business indicators were found, but naming should be reviewed by the community before public display.

ENTITY RELATIONSHIP: JRSA (founded 2014, coalition coordinated by Australian Red Cross) is distinct from but closely tied to Tiraapendi Wodli, the Aboriginal community-led JR project in Port Adelaide that JRSA + Red Cross support. The "73 children" and "$53.1m / 15 partnerships" figures are PRF national portfolio figures (Tiraapendi Wodli is one of the partners), not JRSA-only outcomes — flagged in as_reported wording. The "50% prison growth" and "$1m/child" figures are context stats cited by these orgs, not JRSA's own program outcomes.

UNVERIFIED: exact JRSA founding-month, current JRSA staff/governance roster, and any JRSA-specific (vs Tiraapendi Wodli / PRF portfolio) outcome metrics — not found in accessible public record.

sites.notes:
CONFIRMED. Website https://justicereinvestment.net.au/ is the org's own site. Logo URL was HTTP-verified (200, image/png, 41,071 bytes) by direct curl.

ENTITY: National (not place-based). JRNA is a First Nations-led national network / community of practice established 2015 (UTS Jumbunna source), now incorporated as Justice Reinvestment Network Australia Ltd, an Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander community-controlled organisation governed by a 100% Aboriginal board. Definition quoted from homepage: "Justice reinvestment is a way of working that shifts power and decision-making to First Nations communities to self-determine the best way to improve justice outcomes in their community."

PEOPLE: 7 directors are publicly self-named with cultural/mob identifications on the org's own About us page, so naming is publicly volunteered and treated as culturally safe. Two Co-Chairs: Devon Cuimara (WA) and Aysha Kerr (Qld). No Sorry Business indicators found. The "National Justice Reinvestment Coordinator" appears to be an Aboriginal Legal Service (NSW/ACT)-hosted role (job listing found) — the individual currently holding it was NOT identified in public sources, so omitted rather than guessed.

IMPACT METRICS — IMPORTANT ATTRIBUTION CAVEAT: The four Maranguka outcome figures ($3.1M saving, 23% DV reduction, 31% Year 12 retention, 42% custody-days reduction) and the $81.5M figure are reported ON JRNA's own page (public-policy-shifts) but describe the Maranguka justice reinvestment project in Bourke (a separate, JRNA-affiliated community initiative led via Just Reinvest NSW) and a 2022 ALP election commitment — NOT JRNA's own organisational outputs. They originate from a KPMG 2018 assessment of Maranguka. Included because they carry a real source_url containing the exact quote, but they are NOT JRNA-as-entity metrics. No quantified impact metric specific to JRNA's own network operations (e.g., member count) was found; the membership count is stated only qualitatively ("members from across Australia"). The public-policy-shifts page has NO visible publication date (most recent content references 2023), so news date = null.

NATIONAL FUNDING CONTEXT (from web search of govt sources, not JRNA's own site, so kept in notes only — not in impact_metrics): National Justice Reinvestment Program launched 2023; National Justice Reinvestment Unit to be established 2024; grant round closed Nov 2024 (near-fully subscribed); from 2026-27 the Australian Government has committed $20M/year for JR initiatives plus an additional $10M over 4 years for place-based JR partnerships in Central Australia (NT). These were not individually source-quoted to a single URL with exact wording so are excluded from impact_metrics.

The "Communities and supporters" page is a placeholder ("This page is currently being updated…check back soon") — no member-community list is currently published on JRNA's own site. JR-affiliated NSW sites (Bourke/Maranguka, Moree, Mt Druitt, Kempsey, Nowra) come from Just Reinvest NSW, a JRNA member, not JRNA directly. Maranguka in Bourke was Australia's first JR initiative, established 2013.

data_quality = moderate: clear entity identity, confirmed website + logo, 7 named leaders with sources, and several sourced figures — but the figures are affiliate/context metrics rather than JRNA's own, and there is no published member roster or org-specific impact data.

Interim Unit ran four roundtables (Perth, Adelaide, Darwin, Cairns) in July-August 2025 to design the National Justice Reinvestment Unit.
